title: What exactly is an LLM?
description: A Large Language Model (LLM) is fundamentally a statistical engine designed for next-token prediction, scaled up with massive amounts of data and compute.

sections.title: The Core Objective: Next-Token Prediction
sections.description: At its most basic level, an LLM takes a sequence of words (or 'tokens') as input and calculates the probability distribution for the next token. By repeatedly predicting the most likely next token and appending it to the input, the model generates coherent text. It is essentially an extremely sophisticated autocomplete.
sections.codeBlock:
# Conceptual representation of an LLM's core loop
def generate_text(prompt, max_tokens, model):
    current_text = prompt
    for _ in range(max_tokens):
        # Model predicts the probability of all possible next tokens
        next_token_probs = model.predict_next_token(current_text)
        
        # Sample the most likely token (e.g., greedy decoding)
        next_token = sample(next_token_probs)
        
        current_text += next_token
        if next_token == '<EOS>':
            break
            
    return current_text

sections.title: Learning Patterns, Not Just Statistics
sections.description: While early language models (like n-grams) relied on simple frequency counting, modern LLMs learn deep structural patterns. To accurately predict the next word in a complex sentence, the model is forced to learn grammar, syntax, reasoning, factual knowledge, and world context implicitly during its training phase.

sections.title: How it Scales: The Transformer
sections.description: The architecture that made LLMs possible is the Transformer. Unlike previous sequential models (like RNNs/LSTMs), Transformers process entire sequences in parallel using the Self-Attention mechanism. This allows the model to weigh the importance of every word in the context window relative to every other word, capturing long-range dependencies efficiently.

sections.title: The Training Pipeline
sections.description:
Creating a modern LLM involves three major stages:

1. Pre-training: The model is trained on a massive corpus of internet text to predict the next word. It learns world knowledge and language structure (creates a Base Model).
2. Supervised Fine-Tuning (SFT): The model is trained on high-quality instruction-response pairs to learn how to act as a helpful assistant rather than a document-completer.
3. Alignment (RLHF/DPO): The model is further fine-tuned to align with human preferences (e.g., being safe, harmless, and helpful) using reinforcement learning.

details: Emergent Abilities: One of the most fascinating aspects of LLMs is that as you scale up the parameters (number of weights) and the training data, models begin to exhibit "emergent abilities"—skills they were never explicitly trained for, such as translating languages, writing code, or performing logical reasoning, simply because those patterns existed in the training data.
